content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Kid-friendly adult movies that are actually good

**Score:** 64
**Body:** So my 4yo is sick and I'll probably be spending the next few days sitting around at home with him. Looking for any decent movies (or even enjoyable bad ones) that I can watch whilst my kid is in the room with me! Doesn't need to be interesting to him, just age-appropriate if he happens to look at it. I'm not fussed at all about swearing, but there can't be any scary scenes or graphic violence/blood/gore. Hella fake fight scenes and gunshots without gore is fine. I'm bored of watching kids movies and Google's only giving me family movies I've already seen. TIA!

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. A League of Their Own. Lotta good humored laughs, a little profanity, fairly harmless. Watched it a long time ago with a girlfriends kids. Grandma winced but we all shared a good hearty chuckle. 
2. Strictly Ballroom

Pride and Prejudice (2005 movie or the BBC miniseries)

That Thing You Do

The King’s Speech


  - Thanks for these! That Thing You Do is definitely going on the watch list
3. The Martian is perfect for this, honestly just a guy problem-solving his way off Mars with minimal gore and actually entertaining to watch while your kid does his thing nearby.
4. Back to the Future
  - Truuue. My son would probably get a kick out of the DeLorean, he was pretty enamoured with the flying car in Flubber 😆
5. Almost every Jackie Chan movie.
6. Project Hail Mary
7. Harry and the Hendersons

Great Outdoors

Adventures in Babysitting

Uncle Buck

School of Rock


8. Do a Steven Spielberg binge, most of them are fine.

## 2. Great thrillers/ psychological films

**Score:** 15
**Body:** Big fan of the works of David fincher (seven + zodiac specifically), the Hannibal TV show and true detective. Looking for anything with similar vibe 🫡

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Frailty. It's good if you haven't seen it. 
  - Yassss!
2. Mare of Easttown 
3. Citizen-X (1995), Manhunter (1986), Prisoners (2013), Bone daddy (1998), Ressurrection (1999), Mindhunters (2004), Kiss the girls (1997) and the follow up Along came a spider (2004), 8mm (1999), Mystic river (2003), Copycat (1995)
  - Nice list!
4. One flew over the cuckoo's nest was really good
5. Prisoners (2013), The Empty Man (2020)
6. Prisoners (2013) by Denis Villeneuve. Same rainy, oppressive atmosphere as Se7en and the investigation structure is incredibly tight. Jake Gyllenhaal is terrifying in it. If you want something with more action but same energy, check out The Killer (2023) — Fincher's latest.
7. Gorky Park (1983)

## 3. Super Solider movies where the super soldiers are made via some form of training, rather than serums or steroids?

**Score:** 15
**Body:** I know this sounds like it doesn't make sense. Because the characters wouldn't necessarily super soldiers, if training is the thing that makes them peak human. But im just curious to see how a Writer can go about doing this though.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Soldier -- late 90s movie starring Kurt Russel might just be what you're looking for.
  - One of my favorite movies and a stealth sequel to Blade Runner! 
  - Kurt Russell and Jason Scott Lee, a very cool movie
2. Soldier (1998)
3. Remo Williams:  The Adventure Begins
  - I tore my fingernail off when I was younger trying to "finger punch" something like an idiot based on that movie 😄
    - You need to build up to that with your finger board.
4. Batman Begins
5. Red Sparrow  
The Bourne movies
6. Equilibrium. Drugs and training in a specific style.

## 4. What is your most ‘out there’ movie rec/fav movie?

**Score:** 14
**Body:** Title is self explanatory, but what is your most off the wall movie you like as a comfort movie, like to share with others, or even has just stuck with you after one watch? 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Dredd 2012
2. RRR
3. Killer Klowns From Outer Space
4. Fear and Loathing in Las Vegas
5. With order:  
Big Fish (2003)  
The Fall (2006)  
Mr. Nobody (2009)
6. Tusk. 
7. the florida project, defenitely not everyone's cup of tea but i loved it
8. Donnie Darko, Mandy, Spring Breakers
9. 'The Holy Mountain' (1973) is a classic
10. Rubber. Just lol...and go, Robert!

## 5. Best movies of any genre set during the broad American colonial period (1565-1783)

**Score:** 13
**Body:** Hi there! I am interested in movies set during the American colonial period. For the purposes of this question, I will define this period as lasting from the founding of St. Augustine in 1565 to the signing of the Treaty of Paris in 1783.

Any genre is absolutely fine, and historicity isn't a requirement. For example, I would gladly accept a suggestion of "VVitch" even though it's a horror film. 

Thanks for your suggestions! 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Last of the Mohicans is always a winner. 
2. The Patriot

1776

John Adams, not a movie, an HBO miniseries

Turn’s pretty good, but kind of fizzled out for me after a few seasons. 

Oddly, there’s not that many. 
  - > Oddly, there’s not that many. 

Since nobody mentioned yet, The New World (2005)
3. The Crucible (1996)
4. Does Apocalypto count?
5. The movie was good but the book was better.   Last of the Mohicans. The Leatherstocking Tales is the complete set of books by James Fenimore Cooper.

Turn the series about American spy ring during the revolution is good also.
6. Here's a few, I hope they're what you had in mind:
Drums Along the Mohawk 1939
The Fighting Kentuckian 1949
The Kentuckian 1955


7. The New World
8. 1776
9. The Scarlet Letter (1995)

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. What’re your thoughts on Brendon Gleeson? for me In Bruges is one of my favorite movies and would no where near be the same without him he’s fantastic in it.

**Score:** 1616

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. He’s always a highlight, such a versatile actor - proven by the images above. In Bruges is one of my favourite films, his chemistry with Colin Farrell is off the charts. They play beautifully off one another and makes the film the delight that it is. It’s endlessly rewatchable.
  - Watch In Bruges and Banshees of Inisherin back to back... 
2. He made Lake Placid the gem it was, along with the indomitable Betty White. 
3. How come The Guard is never on these
  - Or Mr. Mercedes.  
4. Good actor, but I believe it's Brendan not Brendon.
5. He's rock solid in everything.

Loved his part in Edge of Tomorrow; felt incredibly believable.
6. He's an incredibly versatile actor, and In Bruges (2008) is one of my favorite films.

https://preview.redd.it/190dwvuyim8h1.jpeg?width=1125&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=46c4e95533b990be21457df3788302ff9eb14431
7. He was great in Kingdom of Heaven as well
8. The General is also a great movie of his. He plays a famous Irish criminal named martin cahill

## 2. What is your favorite drinking scene?

**Score:** 740
**Body:** Jaws

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|Tbj1X2js8WPOE)
2. ![gif](giphy|xYZuDzilLt9yU)
3. ![gif](giphy|DrdANwYq2lMgE)

Waitress, bring us a pitcher of beer ever 15 minutes ‘til one of us passes out….then bring one every 10. 

Back to School
5. ![gif](giphy|qEaZxVLkkMnK0)
  - ![gif](giphy|YrkD2MwU8J382Krrxt)  
Masterclass 
6. ![gif](giphy|3oKHWBy6GFcLdEhH0Y)
7. ![gif](giphy|v8QKcsEoYsXG8)
8. https://i.redd.it/zt1j7yml6q8h1.gif
9. ![gif](giphy|3o85xzYIHuDl00se3K)

## 3. Really enjoyed No Hard Feelings. It's a lighthearted comedy with plenty of humor and heart. Jennifer Lawrence was great in it, and her chemistry with Andrew Barth Feldman was one of the film's biggest strengths. What are your thoughts on it?

**Score:** 708

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The maneater piano solo was the best scene in the whole movie. 
  - Idk, when he prematurely came on her thigh I thought I had witnessed peak cinema 
  - I first saw Jennifer Lawrence in a movie called Winters Bone. I was so impressed by her performance that I looked up her name because I knew she was going to be big. The very real emotions she conveyed in that Maneater scene is why cinema is an art. You can absolutely feel her shame and self awareness of her own actions connecting to the words in the song. That’s what a artist can do. They can share the real feelings of a character that never was. Not many can take you to those places so completely. 
2. AND STAY THE FUCK OUTTA MONTAUK! 
3. I laughed *so hard* during the beach fight omg
  - She does not get enough credit for how great she is as a comedic actress, I could not breathe during this scene because I was laughing so hard 
  - Is this when she’s full frontal nudity and she hits perfectly excited belly to back suplex?
    - Suplex City, bitch!
4. I didn't expect to like this one so much! I thought it may be gross but it was surprisingly sweet. I also love the actors who play his parents 
5. I loved that they didn’t end up together. Great movie

## 4. "You should move to a small town where the rule of law still exists. You will not survive here. You are not a wolf. And this is the land of wolves now" - Sicario

**Score:** 367

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I love this movie, brutal and intelligent. Time for a rewatch.
  - Sign the contract. 
2. "you're asking me how a watch works..for now, just keep an eye on the time"
3. Fantastic movie.

Second one was nowhere near as insightful, but still cool.
4. What is true on a large scale is true on a small scale.
5. Denis Villeneuve is the best director working right now and I'll fight anyone who disagrees. Flawless filmography. Dude does not miss. 
  - Was having a conversation with someone about DV on Reddit and they called Sicario an Ad for trumps wall…   
![gif](giphy|LyJ6KPlrFdKnK)
  - He really made this film what it is too, with a big assist from Del Toro. The script in its original state, which you can find, is not like the movie at all and frankly explains why the second is bad. Taylor Sheridan is a good writer but benefited hugely by not having creative control of this film.
6. "Don't ever point a weapon at me again" has always been a quote, and that scene, that has always stuck with me, nothing truly special about it but it just feels so real, a good example of a true soldier I guess?
7. Didn't think you would.

https://preview.redd.it/iaztl6co9q8h1.jpeg?width=1600&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=d970f8b08befa884345cab0d0a45a2923992d270

## 5. CGI de-aging has absolutely no room in cinema. Prove me wrong.

**Score:** 216
**Body:** Digital aging and de-aging has never truly worked, and it continues to ruin movies. No matter how many hundreds of millions of dollars a studio spends, the technology always falls into the uncanny valley. Even if the face looks decent static, the human brain instantly flags the unnatural eye movements, expressions etc.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. https://preview.redd.it/2012rx5sum8h1.jpeg?width=622&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=5809f0bf424395f9b8f8417b778d6b38251b28cc

  
Tell that to young Mike Meyers in Love Guru
  - Bloody love this 😂
    - Best reference ever 😂 worst movie ever!
2. For flashback that are minutes long max I’m okay with. Not a fan of it for a big chunk or majority of a film. 

Saying that I think they did in a bit in Spider-Man: No Way Home and it wasn’t off putting. 
  - Marvel has used it best so far as a way to show convincing flashbacks without trying to tell large chunks of a story à la The Irishman. 
  - have a look at the third X-Men where Xavier and Magneto visit young Phoenix

they only de-aged them about 15 years though, but that looked really good, especially considering the time
3. I will never mind a little of it for flashbacks and such. But if you need it for your entire performance, maybe they should hire another actor.

I am exempting Paul Reubens' use of it for the final Pee Wee Herman film. Nobody wanted to see an old Pee Wee Herman and nobody else could ever play the part.


4. I agree. In my opinion The Irishman really suffered because of it. So insistent on the face looking "younger" but the body doesn't lie lol De Niro still moving like an older man. 
5. The greatest de-aging was Frank Costanza in the Korean War flashback 
6. Benjamin Button has a response 

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. I watched Bill Paxton's 2001 film Frailty. I really appreciate how much it messes with the audience.

**Score:** 79
**Body:** # ⚠️BIG SPOILER WARNING⚠️

Frailty is such a thought provoking thriller. It presents itself as a mental health/generational trauma drama. It's honestly kind of predictable for like 90% of the film, but it's enjoyable to see because the actors allow you to experience the religious trauma that leads to an eventual serial killer. It's interesting to watch, with certain aspects of the father/son relationship feeling strangely relatable. You know the dad is insane, but anybody who's had complex family dynamics knows how hard it can be to talk to a loved one about their mental instability. There's also a powerless feeling where even when Fenton wants to turn in the Dad that he might be murdered himself. It succeeds at portraying an understanding but critical look at how a person with a good head on their shoulders succumbs to madness.

And then the ending happens. It turns out everything that the dad has been saying the entire movie was true. He really does get visions from God. Him and his son are not only actually brutally murdering demons, but God is helping them on their mission. Now the serial killer son is 100% an actual demon and the empathy felt for his situation is turned on its head.

Its such a thought provoking ending. At first I was a bit taken aback that the mental health/generational trauma centered movie performed a 180 out of nowhere, but it has slowly added to my appreciation of the movie the longer I've sat with it. Sure, the dad was objectively correct, but that's almost more horrifying than the initial mental health/generational trauma angle the film was setting up. God is using human beings like pawns to basically act as serial killers. Its as if the craziest cult you had ever heard of that looked like it was the byproduct of untreated mental illness was actually true, and that's more horrifying than had it been mental illness.

Disturbing stuff. Would recommend. What do you all think of Frailty?

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. *Frailty* is one of the best and most assuredly under seen and under discussed horror thrillers. The script is masterful, and the cast is firing on all cylinders. I won’t say it’s underrated. It’s not. Most everyone who sees it acknowledges that it is top notch. But it definitely deserves to be brought up way more often than it is. 

It’s a shame Paxton never helmed another one, and we lost him as an actor too early as well. 

I guess he’ll go down with other famous single film directors. Laughton w/ Night of the Hunter, Walter Murch with Return to Oz (so SO strange),
Brando’s One-Eyed Jacks, and Gary Oldman’s Nil by Mouth.
  - Not to be that guy but Bill Paxton directed *The Greatest Game Ever Played* in 2005. 

But yeah, his direction is great in *Frailty*. My one complaint is the last 10 minutes has like 10 twists and it gets a little hard to follow. You have Matthew McConaughey's character reveal, the supernatural reveal, the secretary reveal, the fbi agent demon reveal, the Fenton demon reveal, and the town sheriff reveal happen within like 5 minutes and it's just a *little* clunky. I wish they had just a little more time to breathe. Not necessarily because of plot reasons but more because I, the viewer, need time to piece it all together. I felt like I was missing details trying to wrap my brain around one twist while they were revealing another. Also I felt some of the visuals and music were a bit over the top for an otherwise grounded movie. These are more nitpicks though. Everything else worked pretty well so I ultimately don't care.
    - > Greatest Game Ever Played

I’d never heard of this. Of course, sports movies aren’t my thing so that’s not surprising. 

But I like Paxton enough to look this up. Thanks for expanding my knowledge. 
2. This was a brilliant, depressing movie. I loved that it fully subverted my expectations *and* that i didn't see it coming. It's pretty rare for that to happen, and when it happened i was very much 'Holy shit'.

It's on my watchlist for 'some time soon'.
3. I could almost make out a short fleeting wave of these types of movies in the late 90s/early 2000s, with Frailty, Stir of Echoes and Frequency. Middle aged protagonist in small town/suburb, grey weather and a supernatural element. I'd categorize them as post-Sixth Sense supernatural domestic Dad-thrillers or Dad mysteries. I equate them as a cousin to the wave of cozy domestic female thrillers that was popular after Gone Girl a few years back.

All of them were good little gems that didn't have high expectations. Frequency being the break-through film.  Frailty maybe being the most memorable for expertly toeing the line between the rational and the supernatural, never really revealing itself even at the end, but instead redirecting that question of belief from Fenton onto the viewer.
  - Fallen (1998) is another one that fits well imho.


Maybe questions a bit what the throughline really is, it doesnt have most of the elements you list, but I feel the vibe is very similar.


I think it might have to do more with how it bleeds from the ordinary into the supernatural, and then maybe just the general tone.
    - I was going to add Fallen to the list as well. 
4. Haven’t seen it yet so I skipped most of your post for spoilers, but if you love Bill Paxton in mind-benders, try *Brain Dead*. It’s one of the only good “which parts were a dream” movies out there.

## 2. Hirokazu Koreeda

**Score:** 60
**Body:** I can’t believe I overlooked this guy. Over the last week I watched six movies by him (Nobody Knows, After Life, Still Walking, Monster, Shoplifters, Maborosi) and every single one was amazing. I was trying to figure out a ranking, but I’d have to think about it a little more. It got me thinking about who is considered the greatest non-American filmmakers. Would you put Hirokazu Koreeda on that list? Who else should make the list? 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Koreeda is 100% on my list of greatest filmmakers, american or not.  he has a very contemplative style that i admire, and i love a filmmaker who is overly sentimental the way he is. my top three from him would probably be Still Walking, Monster, and Nobody Knows. 
2. He's my favorite contemporary Japanese director. Check out I Wish when you get a chance. It's a personal favorite of mine. Hamaguchi, Kiyoshi Kurosawa, Sho Miyake, and Chie Hayakawa are all great as well.   
  
He'd absolutely be on that list for me. If we're talking Asian directors, I'd also add Edward Yang, Lee Chang-Dong, Jia Zhangke, Jafar Panahi, and Wong Kar-wai.
3. Kore-eda is incredible. Monster was my favorite film that year. Have you seen any Hamaguchi? I think you might dig his work. he hasn't been at it as long as Kore, but he has something very special too. 
4. Really wish Criterion or another label would release more of his work. It’s frustratingly hard to find many of his films outside of Japan. Some are unreleased, out of print, or only available on old DVDs.

A proper box set would make his work much easier to discover for new fans.
5. Obviously, Akira Kurosawa, Yosijoru Ozu, Satyajit  Ray, Ingmar Bergman, Andrei Tarkovsky, Fellini, Luis Bunuel, Robert Bresson are some of the heavyweights from the earlier times. 



Abbas Kiarostami’s filmography is stacked with Close-Up, The Koker Trilogy, The Wind Will Carry Us among others.


A name that deserves much more recognition would be Ritwit Ghatak imo, he was truly ahead of his times with films like Ajantrik, Megha Dhaka Tara, Titas etc.


Coming to more recent filmmakers with a filmography spanning several films - Bong joon ho, Park Chan Wook, Jia Zhangke, Jafar Panahi.


The Taiwan New Wave directors Edward Yang, Hou Hsiao Hsein 



I would like to the also mention the big three mexicans - Del Toro, Innaritu, and Cuaron despite them having made works in English.



Agnes Varda and Werner Herzog are my personal favourites so sneaking them in here as well.


Jan Svankmajer has a filmography that can measure against anyone if you’re into stopmotion animation.


Miyazaki’s name has to come up in the discussion as well ig.



I’m sure I’m missing some names but I’ve tried to put most artists whose works I’ve loved.
6. Ah you’re in for a treat because he’s super prolific and doesn’t make bad movies. There’s at least 10 more narrative features to see, as well as a couple mini-series and docs. Distance, Hana, After the Storm, I Wish, Our Little Sister are all lesser seen but equally great ones. Like Father, Like Son is one of his most beloved. Broker and The Truth are two films where he stretched outside of his native Japan in cool and interesting ways. He’s without a doubt one of my favorite living filmmakers. 
7. I loved Shoplifters and Nobody Knows. Maborosi is good as well, but the pacing seemed a bit too slow for me.

Air Doll, however, felt too dark and hopeless. I liked it, but it left me with a feeling of sadness and unease that seemed somewhat out of tone with the director's other works, as if the last part of the movie had been directed by someone else.
8. Yeah, love his work! 

I discovered him accidently at a film festival that screened After the Storm. It resonated so well that I couldn't stop obsessing over his style. Still Walking might be my favorite of his films, but After will always have that special place in my heart. 
9. Welcome to the Koreeda fanclub. I discovered him during pandemic. And Maborosi was life changing for me. I was fortunate enough to meet him in-person and listening to him talk a few years back at Malaysia Film Festival!
10. My Koreeda ranking:  
  
Our Little Sister  
Monster  
Shoplifters  
Still Walking  
Nobody Knows  
Like Father Like Son  
The Truth  
After the Storm  
The Third Murder  
Broker  
Maborosi  
I Wish  
Air Doll  
Afterlife  
Sheep in the Box  
  
Some of the better international filmmakers right now - Pawel Pawlikowski, Andrey Zvyagintsav, Carla Simon, Sergei Loznitsa, Aki Kaurismaki, Nuri Bilge Ceylan




## 3. Disclosure Day — almost great, and that almost really hurts.

**Score:** 24
**Body:** Okay so I went in absolutely buzzing. Spielberg. UFOs. Emily Blunt.

I mean come on, that's a lineup that makes you feel like a kid again before the lights even go down. The trailers had me convinced this was going to be something special, and honestly for the first act I thought it might be.

&#x200B;

And look Emily Blunt is flat out incredible in this. The scene where she freezes mid-weather report and starts clicking out alien sounds? That's the kind of unhinged, committed performance you just don't see enough of. She's funny, chaotic, and genuinely moving all at once. She carries so much of this film on her shoulders and absolutely does not drop it.

&#x200B;

Spielberg's eye is still immaculate too. Some of the compositions in this movie are just tossed off like nothing, shots that other directors would storyboard for weeks. The Kaminski backlighting, the way scenes move through space, you feel the craft constantly.

&#x200B;

And I get what he's going for. The empathy angle, the idea that understanding the unknown starts with understanding yourself. That's genuinely beautiful thematically.

&#x200B;

But the movie just doesn't stick the landing. The third act lurches into this desperate feel-good catharsis that the film hasn't really earned. It's like Spielberg knew exactly what he wanted to say but couldn't figure out how to make you \*feel\* it. You leave understanding the message and forgetting the movie.

&#x200B;

Really wanted more. Blunt deserved more.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Nothing hurts more than a piece of art you want to love but only end up liking.

Theres tons of mediocre works and I don't mind. But a piece of art that genuinely tries but slips up just before reaching greatness? It burns me so much. 
2. I found it to be pretty terrible mostly due to the script. The bad guys are perhaps the dumbest group of people to ever exist in film. How they managed to keep aliens a secret for so long is beyond belief. Then you have Bono’s daughter and her mother superior who show up as Spielberg’s take on religion instead if character. The film aims to be a two-handed between Margaret and Daniel but the film seemed to get tired of Daniel and his non-cinematic power. Which brings us to the plot of the movie. Aliens abuse two children and give them powers so that decades later they might get together to show the world that aliens exist. Okay, so maybe the aliens are dumber than the bad guys here. Why not, just show themselves to humanity?
  - Hahaha the alien is spot on,  if they've been planning this for decades, abusing kids to create messengers, why not just land on the White House lawn? The bad guys are incompetent but the aliens aren't much smarter.

Agree on Daniel too, film completely abandoned him halfway through.
  - This right here. The antagonist just didnt work. Weakest writing in the movie. And the empathy angle? I kinda see it but weak execution as well bcs the movie focused more on the mystery about the 2 characters...and it did not pay off
  - Nailed it. I thought this movie was shockingly awful. So much of this made zero sense. Emily Blunt was doing her best but damn. 
3. I thought the final 40 mins were great. But also those final 40 mins were given away during the trailers. And at that point I was so pissed off about everything else leading up to that point that anything emotional I just couldn’t connect with. Let alone Colin Firth just stopping as soon as he gets there. Then what’s the point of the last 2 hours? Just a lame thriller where everything works out to move the plot forward. Just a complete mess of a movie. 
4. I didn't enjoy it. It felt messy to me, like it was trying to say something really big but couldn't decide exactly what it was lol. 

I didn't understand the obvious religious angle that was randomly thrown in. That all just felt really unnecessary and again like it was just trying to say something but idk what. 

At times I was really bored, all the chases were just whatever to me because the suspension wasn't there, I never thought there was a chance they would be caught. 

I basically checked out at the scene of Emily Blunt reading everyone's mind to get out of the hangar. That was goofy imo. 
  - The "trying to say something big but couldn't decide what" is such a perfect description honestly 
The chase scenes suffer exactly because of what you said :  zero tension. You never once believe they're actually in danger. 
5. I agree with what you said but the real problem I found in the third act is how the movie doesn't take any strong stance. See Contact with Jodie Foster, they are bold in their message, what to expect from humanity, aliens... here I didn't find anything
6. I'll paste what i put in the movies thread

It's baaaad. Soulless and flat, lacking anything resembling a vision. Characters without personality who exist only to spew trailer bait lines. We are bombarded endlessly with music and lens flares and camera movement and cuts, never afforded any time to breathe, despite very little actually happening, with a plot largely expressed through speeches about how important things are that fail to convince. A muddy tone that can't decide between pulpy adventure and high intellect, so ends up being neither. Overstuffed and overlong, a decent movie buried beneath abysmal character writing, poorly executed clichés, a generationally phoned in Colin Firth performance, an entirely unnecessary character and secondary plot involving Josh O'Connor, and mind numbing hyperstimulous cinematography and editing.

At no point did I feel this movie was made by humans, a prototype for the kind of AI movies we have to look forward to. Lacking any kind of artistic vision, any teeth, or even any characteristic flaws. A smooth, plastic piece of noise pollution.

The third act almost saves it. The scenes in the TV studio show flashes of Spielberg, strong direction and strong performances cutting through the sludge. Ultimately though, the film ends on an anti climax. I understand why, and in a stronger film it would have worked, but ending a prolonged bowel movement with a cut to black doesn't suddenly grant artistic weight to the shit, despite the film's best intentions.

I think of movies like Jaws, the indie films, Jurassic Park. Even though I don't like the latter, I can appreciate that there is humanity there, a strong execution elevating a simple idea through the humanity of the man behind the camera. There is grit, there is soul, there are flaws. The camera holds on people because it trusts that we empathize with them without needing to punctuate their existence with endless cuts and flair.

No animals were harmed in the making of this movie, and no humans were involved at all.

## 4. IJW: The skin I live in [2011]

**Score:** 20
**Body:** So I just finished watching this movie. I need to talk about it because it really blew my mind.

I didn't know what to expect and honestly I wasn't ready for where the story went. At first it felt like an European thriller. A great surgeon, a mysterious woman locked in his house very classy very calm.

Then slowly it starts to show its true self and by the end I was just sitting there thinking... What did I just see.

The darkest theme in the movie is mens ego. Robert Ledgard is one of the characters I've seen in a while because he truly thinks he's right in everything he does.

His daughter gets hurt and instead of dealing with that pain like a person he turns it into this huge project of control and revenge.

The punishment he gives is much worse than the crime that at some point you forget who the original victim was.

And that's of the point I think.

What really got to me was the Stockholm syndrome part.

The captive slowly starts to feel emotionally connected to the man who ruined her life and the director, Almodóvar doesn't show it as weakness.

It's more disturbing than that.

It's, about survival.

It's what people do when they have no choice.

That's the part that really stays with you.

There's also this mixing of identity, body control and agreement that the movie never lets you feel safe with.

Every time you think you've found a moral ground the movie takes it away.

It's not a watch but its absolutely worth it.

Maybe don't watch it alone at night.

Let me know your thoughts. 

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I'm so glad to see this thread because it is such an insane movie and it seems like most people barely know about it. I saw it almost by accident, had no idea what it was about, and now I'd say it's maybe the most bizarre story I've seen presented in a relatively normal narrative form (maybe a bit of a soap-opera style), and the wildest part is that it somehow works. If I just read the plot, I'd think it's crazy but would also assume I would just not be able to take it seriously. Somehow this movie, as over the top as it is, made me fully buy into the story. 


>The punishment he gives is much worse than the crime that at some point you forget who the original victim was.

The guy never even committed any crime, the daughter just freaked out because she was mentally fucked up due to her mom trauma (the mutilated mom is also a good horror side element to the story.) The dad walked in and thinks he raped her, but she was just having a meltdown. 

I generally find the physical mutilation horror very effective, even movies that don't take themselves too seriously like Tusk are able to viscerally resonate. 

What is so genius in this movie is that it is also a very disturbing physical mutilation horror, but the end result (that you see at the start) is a very attractive woman. Where the movie blew my mind was that when I realized who she is, my perception of her genuinely changed and I felt almost a similar horror as if I was watching someone being turned into a walrus or flayed alive while looking at a hot actress. And the scenes where you finally see what happened in the past are legitimately terrifying.

Aside from that, the fact that the doctor fell inlove with the guy he thinks raped his daughter the moment he made him look like his mutilated wife (pre-mutilation) was insane but brilliant. So much of how we perceive a person is by what we see. His ex wife no longer looks like herself, the perceived rapist (although Banderas is wrong about the situation) looks like the ex wife, so he really just becomes a better version of her and all of the feelings Banderas had for her shift on to him without even stopping to think how bizarre it all is. 
As a viewer, you also can't look at her and think she is that guy, but you also stop being able to look at her and think it's just a hot girl. That makes her something disturbing without any need for special effects. 

The movie starts at the point where you naturally perceive their dynamic as if she was just his wife he is doing some skin surgery on, just to totally shift the perception, that is done so well. i don't even know how it managed to pull it off. I think that the soapy tone kind of helped, being too realistic and serious in other aspects could clash with what we need to accept, but it's still interesting that it didn't create a non-serious tone at all either, I found it psychologically convincing.

It's also interesting to think what the guy should do in the end. On one hand he is mutilated for life and could probably kill himself, on the other hand, to the world, he is socially in the best position imaginable.

## 5. No Country For Old Men - The Characterization of Sheriff Bell

**Score:** 16
**Body:** I just rewatched this film for the first time in years, after just finished the Cormac McCarthy novel this last week. And to be honest, I'm a bit bothered by how Bell is portrayed in the film. It really feels like he gives not one single shit in the film, regularly avoiding meetups investigating, to the point where is deputy is palpably annoyed by his inaction. Compared to his portrayal in the novel, he felt very active and dedicated, and yet just fundamentally one step behind Chigurh at every step, til at the end it seemed like his character was truly defeated. As if Bell gave it his literal all, and that it still wasn't enough. 

  
This frustration is exacerbated by the ending being so closely linked to the novel's ending - a retired Bell describing a dream of his father leading him through the woods. In the novel, this passage carries heavy thematic significance - the lineage of being a boy into an old man who has grown too old for the world as it is now. That is the significance of the title - that the world will change under our feet as we age, and that will leave us in a world we no longer understand. And to me, after playing the same ending after characterizing Bell totally differently from the novel, the ending seems so hollow and borderline forced compared to the novel, which is reminiscent of Chigurh's final speech to Carla Jean, that everything lead to that very moment and that there was no avoiding it.

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Bell is overwhelmed, bewildered, and frankly terrified by evils of the world. It isn't just Chigurh, as evidenced by the opening monologue where he talks about the boy that killed a girl just to see what it was like and promised to do it again, and the scene in the diner where he reads excerpts from the newspaper article about the people being kept in a dungeon with dog collars.

He is the reason the title is what it is. That as he ages and gets more philosophical, and has more existential ennui, he is just totally out of his element trying to enforce the law against lawless psychotics and gang/cartel warfare. This is definitely portrayed in a thinner way in the movie, since we don't get the benefit of the extended characterisation, but that's somewhat inevitable when you are adapating a novel where the majority of the characterisation comes through via first person narration. However I disagree that his character arc is assassinated, it's just a simplified version of it: he is not apathetic and unbothered, he IS doing his best, he just fundamentally cannot understand the mindset and the reality of the people he is dealing with which makes him feel helpless to combat and outwit them.
  - The title is from Yeats 
    - It is, yes. ?
      - Sailing to Byzantium. First stanza.

That is no country for old men. The young  
In one another's arms, birds in the trees,  
—Those dying generations—at their song,  
The salmon-falls, the mackerel-crowded seas,  
Fish, flesh, or fowl, commend all summer long  
Whatever is begotten, born, and dies.  
Caught in that sensual music all neglect  
Monuments of unageing intellect.
        - Haha yes I know. I was just wondering if you mentioned it for any particular reason?
  - You’re getting at something that I nearly mentioned in the post, with the part about the boy that swore he would kill that girl. In the novel, that (and so much of Bell’s dialogue in the film) takes the place of these almost memoir like headers at the start of each chapter. That gives the novel the sense that Bell is in fact the “main character” and that Moss/Chigurh’s story are fundamentally interlinked to his, or are a segue to tell Bell’s story. Whereas in the film, it often felt to me that Bell served more in a nearly expository way - I rarely got the feeling that he was pulling the emotional core of the story.

I do concede what you’re saying about it being borderline impossible to purely adapt his character into film, and that the core nature of his character is held in tact. But I feel like they could’ve angled him just slightly better.
    - I would add that in the book, Bell “fails” when he decides not to enter the hotel room at the end. In the movie, they made the key change that he DOES enter the room. I think the reason for that is partially to balance the idea that if you don’t spend time in Bell’s head like you do in the novel, it might SEEM like he doesn’t give a shit.
  - One thought I'm having, is that I might be falling victim to comparison here. Moss and Chigurh are adapted so perfectly in a 1-to-1 sense into the film, that I simply expected Bell to be as well, but I failed to realize the fundamental difference in the amount of internal depth that the novel gives Bell and not the other two characters, and how this would actually translate through to film.
2. I'm not going to repeat everything the other user said, but I'll add something.

Tommy Lee Jones is from Texas. West Texas, I believe. His demeanor matches what the character in the book was attempting to capture. As amazing of a writer as McCarthy is, Jones' acting as a West Texan is simply truer. It's more natural, and therefore the character doesn't announce his feelings. It's portrayed in a more subtle manner, whereas literature, you need to describe all of it.

As far as Bell caring, his actual actions aren't changed much from the book, so if you're not seeing he gives a shit through his action beats, you're not paying attention to what he's doing. Everybody in the story regards him, so you know he's been a solid sheriff for a long time. He doesn't investigate things he already knows, which also shows how seasoned and good he is at his job. (This is contrasted by his deputy). When you're that old and good, you don't take extra steps. You try to head the bad guys off at the pass, which is what he spends the movie trying to do, only to be a couple minutes late. Old man, slowing down.

To me, the ending of the film hits the same note as the novel. The difference is it's Tommy Lee Jones providing a masterful portrayal, and not the sheriff person I imagined in my head (with no real life reference to West Texas or the people who have lived there for a century). Still, the message and feeling hits the same for me.
3. My sense of Sheriff Bell wasn’t that he wasn’t trying or had given up but rather that the reality of the evil he was facing was beyond his ability to understand. I am at an age (59) where I am beginning to understand the sense that world is passing you by. This theme is explored in a few conversations throughout the movie culminating in the statement that I will sum up as “what’s coming isn’t all for you. That’s vanity”. His explanation to his wife about his dreams feels to me like an understanding/ acceptance of the truth of this. I don’t see his struggle as giving up or not caring but rather his trying to come to terms with the world as it is.
